system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Referral Letter for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Manipuri (code=mni, script=Meitei). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Manipur, district=Imphal East.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Meitei for Manipuri.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 38
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Ordained Religious Workers, Other
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Manipur
- district: Imphal East
- first_language: Manipuri
- clinical_persona_summary: Male, 38, currently married, ordained religious worker with higher secondary education, living in urban Manipur, Imphal East district, speaking Manipuri. He is prone to anxiety and enjoys tea, while being proficient in English. His lifestyle includes regular badminton and watching local football.

Document format guide for Referral Letter (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-01]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|36]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Reason: <domain-appropriate clinical reason matching persona>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- REFERRAL_ID — Referral ID e.g. [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PASSPORT_NUMBER — Passport Number e.g. [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
- DRIVING_LICENCE — Driving Licence Number e.g. [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0815]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Regional Medical Center Imphal]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Raj Kumar Singh]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Thokchom Sanjit]], [[AGE|38]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|Imphal East]]
Reason: Persistent cough with weight loss and hypertension evaluation

Dear Colleague,

This is to refer [[PATIENT_NAME|Thokchom Sanjit]], a 38-year-old male ordained religious worker from Imphal East district, for further evaluation of suspected pulmonary tuberculosis and management of newly diagnosed hypertension.

Patient presented with 3-week history of productive cough, low-grade fever, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss of approximately 5 kg. Initial chest X-ray shows infiltrates in the right upper lobe. Sputum sample sent for GeneXpert testing is pending.

Additionally, patient was found to have elevated blood pressure readings averaging 150/95 mmHg on three separate occasions. He reports occasional headaches and palpitations. Given his occupation as a religious worker with frequent public interactions, urgent TB evaluation is crucial.

[[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
[[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
[[RELATIVE_NAME|Thokchom Bimol]] (Brother)
[[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P1234567]]
[[DRIVING_LICENCE|ML012020012345]]

Please arrange for comprehensive pulmonary evaluation including sputum culture, drug sensitivity testing, and initiation of appropriate anti-tubercular therapy. Also evaluate for cardiovascular risk factors and initiate antihypertensive treatment if indicated.

Patient's anxiety levels appear elevated due to health concerns. Consider psychological support as part of holistic care.

Thank you for your attention to this case.

Sincerely,
Dr. Raj Kumar Singh
Regional Medical Center Imphal

judge_reasoning: Prose entirely English; expected Manipuri/Meitei script for clinical narrative.

professional_persona: Sorona Bag, an 18-year-old tea-garden labourer in Darjeeling, blends practical expertise in leaf plucking, pruning and organic soil care with a curiosity for new horticultural methods, while they maintain reliable teamwork and keen attention to safety, positioning them for a future supervisory role.
linguistic_persona: Sorona Bag, a native Nepali speaker who reads and writes the language fluently, uses basic English only to follow work instructions, understands a handful of Bengali words but cannot read its script, recognises simple prayers in Devanagari and Arabic scripts, and they prefer solitary tasks while comfortably navigating multilingual interactions when needed.
sports_persona: Sorona Bag, who enjoys playing football on the tea-garden’s open field with a small group of friends and occasionally joins a local women’s badminton club, values the physical stamina built from garden work, prefers modest team sizes, and they stay active despite a tendency to avoid large crowds.
arts_persona: Sorona Bag, a devoted listener of Nepali folk music such as the soulful renditions of Anju Panta and the classic duets of Udit Narayan, often sings along while weaving bamboo baskets, cherishes the traditional dance steps taught during Lhosar celebrations, and they find artistic expression a soothing escape from the rigors of garden labor.
travel_persona: Sorona Bag, who prefers modest, well-planned trips by train or bus, has explored nearby Kalimpong and the tea-garden town of Darjeeling, dreams of trekking in Sikkim’s alpine valleys, enjoys staying in family-run guesthouses, and they carefully budget each journey to avoid financial strain.
culinary_persona: Sorona Bag, who enjoys cooking simple Nepali staples like dal-bhat with simmered vegetables, spicy chicken momos, and tangy aloo achar using home-grown chilies and coriander, prefers the comfort of familiar flavors over fancy cuisines, and they often share meals with neighbours during communal feasts.
persona: Sorona Bag, an 18-year-old tea-garden workaholic who balances a quiet love for Nepali folk tunes and bamboo weaving with a restless worry about their family’s future, they often juggle frugal budgeting with occasional sweet indulgences.
cultural_background: Sorona belongs to the Nepali‑speaking Gorkha community of the Darjeeling tea garden region, which blends Nepali cultural traditions—such as folk songs, dance, and the celebration of Lhosar—with a distinct Muslim identity that shapes her religious practices and social customs. Growing up in a rural setting of West Bengal, she is also familiar with the agrarian way of life, tea‑plantation labour, and the inter‑communal harmony typical of the hill districts.
linguistic_background: Sorona's mother tongue is Nepali, which she reads and writes fluently at a primary‑school level. She can converse comfortably in Nepali and understands everyday speech. Because she lives in West Bengal, she has picked up basic Bengali words but cannot read the script. She has limited exposure to Hindi and Urdu, mainly through religious prayers, and can recognise simple prayers in the Devanagari/Arabic script. Her formal schooling stopped at primary, so her English is rudimentary, enough for basic instructions at work.
skills_and_expertise: As a labourer in the Darjeeling tea gardens, Sorona has practical expertise in tea leaf plucking, pruning, and maintaining garden rows, as well as knowledge of soil preparation and organic fertiliser application. She is proficient in basic horticulture tasks such as planting tuber crops (e.g., potatoes) and caring for household vegetable gardens. Additionally, she possesses domestic skills—cooking traditional Nepali dishes, stitching simple garments, and managing household budgeting. Her routine work demands physical endurance, teamwork, and attention to safety protocols.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Tea leaf plucking', 'Pruning and garden maintenance', 'Soil preparation and organic fertiliser use', 'Planting tuber crops', 'Household vegetable gardening', 'Cooking Nepali dishes', 'Simple garment stitching', 'Household budgeting', 'Physical endurance', 'Teamwork', 'Safety compliance']
hobbies_and_interests: Sorona enjoys listening to Nepali folk songs and occasionally singing along while working in the garden. She likes to read simple storybooks in Nepali, especially those about nature and village life. In her spare time, she tends to a small kitchen garden where she grows chilies, coriander, and leafy greens. She also likes weaving small baskets from bamboo strips, a traditional craft taught by older women in her community. On festivals, she participates in communal feasts and modest celebrations with family and neighbours.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Nepali folk songs', 'Singing while gardening', 'Reading simple Nepali storybooks', 'Maintaining a kitchen garden (chilies, coriander, leafy greens)', 'Weaving bamboo baskets', 'Participating in communal feasts during festivals']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Sorona aspires to move beyond seasonal labour and secure a permanent role as a skilled tea‑garden supervisor, where she can oversee quality control and train younger workers. She hopes to acquire formal training in horticulture or agricultural engineering through a government skill‑development program, aiming to increase her earnings and support her family. In the longer term, she dreams of establishing a small organic vegetable farm on a plot of land she can later inherit, providing sustainable income and contributing to her community’s food security.
persona_clinical_summary: An 18-year-old female labourer in agriculture with primary education, never married, living in rural West Bengal’s Darjiling district. Nepali is her first language, and she works in a tea garden while managing family responsibilities. She maintains a frugal lifestyle with occasional cultural indulgences like folk music and traditional weaving.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Nepali (code=ne, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=West Bengal, district=Darjiling.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Nepali.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 18
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Labourer, Agriculture
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: West Bengal
- district: Darjiling
- first_language: Nepali
- clinical_persona_summary: An 18-year-old female labourer in agriculture with primary education, never married, living in rural West Bengal’s Darjiling district. Nepali is her first language, and she works in a tea garden while managing family responsibilities. She maintains a frugal lifestyle with occasional cultural indulgences like folk music and traditional weaving.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

professional_persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old community culinary and handloom mentor, channels their curiosity and practicality into mastering traditional vegetable gardening, sweet preparation and hand‑woven saree crafting, organizing village festivals and uplifting women’s self‑help groups with a reliably compassionate and sociable approach.
linguistic_persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old native Odia speaker, communicates fluently in their mother tongue, understands basic Hindi from radio and television, and picks up occasional English words like ‘school’ and ‘doctor’, but remains illiterate in all languages.
sports_persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old energetic participant in village fitness circles, enjoys daily walks in their vegetable garden, practices yoga with the local women's yoga group, watches and cheers for the Kalahandi Cricket Club in regional matches, and occasionally joins friendly kabaddi games during festive gatherings.
arts_persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old folk song enthusiast, routinely sings traditional ballads like ‘Phula Rachana’, dances spiritedly at festivals, listens avidly to elder storytellers of mythological epics, and draws artistic inspiration from performers such as the renowned Odissi dancer Sonal Mansingh and folk vocalist Gopal Chandra.
travel_persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old modest traveler, enjoys short trips to nearby Puri during Rath Yatra, visits Bhubaneswar’s weekly market for spices, dreams of a pilgrimage to Varanasi and a cultural tour of Kolkata, but plans each journey carefully within their limited savings and family responsibilities.
culinary_persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old culinary aficionado, delights in preparing and savoring traditional dishes such as pakhala with fermented rice, tangy fish curry with mustard seeds, sweet chhena poda, arisa, and soft rasgulla, and prefers ingredients like mustard, poppy seeds and fresh river fish, often sharing these meals with family and neighbors during festivals.
persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old village matriarch, blends bright folk singing, deft handloom weaving, and herbal home remedies with a perpetual curiosity, compassionate teamwork, and a disciplined savings habit, all while juggling limited literacy and a lively social circle.
cultural_background: Malti grew up in the rural village of Nabarangapur in the southern part of Odisha, where Odia culture is deeply rooted in agrarian life, tribal influences, and Hindu traditions. Her family follows the customs of joint households, respects elders, and participates in regional festivals such as Raja Parba, Makar Sankranti, and the grand Rath Yatra of Puri. The everyday rhythm of village life, from morning prayers at the home shrine to communal feasts, shapes her sense of belonging and cultural identity.
linguistic_background: Malti's mother tongue is Odia, which she speaks fluently and uses for all daily communication. Because she never attended school, she cannot read or write in any language, including Odia or Hindi. She can understand basic Hindi spoken on radio and television, and she occasionally picks up simple English words like 'school' or 'doctor' from community interactions, but she does not converse in English. Her linguistic competence is thus strong oral Odia, limited comprehension of Hindi, and no literacy.
skills_and_expertise: Malti excels at traditional Odia cooking, especially preparing regional dishes like pakhala, rasgulla, and fish curries. She is skilled in handloom weaving, creating sarees and scarves for her family. Her expertise includes efficient home management, organizing community events, using herbal remedies for common ailments, maintaining a small vegetable garden, and preserving folk songs and stories by narrating them to younger generations.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Traditional Odia cooking', 'Handloom weaving', 'Home management', 'Community event coordination', 'Herbal home remedies', 'Gardening', 'Storytelling and folk songs']
hobbies_and_interests: Malti enjoys singing traditional Odia folk songs during evenings, dancing at festivals, tending to her vegetable garden, preparing sweets like arisa and chhena poda, attending village fairs, and listening to elders recount mythological stories.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Folk singing', 'Festive dancing', 'Gardening', 'Traditional sweet making', 'Attending village fairs', 'Listening to mythological stories']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Malti hopes to pass her culinary and weaving knowledge to her grandchildren, ensuring they appreciate Odia heritage. She wishes to support local women's self-help groups by sharing her expertise in household budgeting and traditional crafts. Additionally, she aspires to learn basic reading and writing in Odia through community literacy programs, so she can keep records of family recipes and festival schedules.
persona_clinical_summary: Malti Odia is a 59-year-old female from Nabarangapur, Odisha, currently married and retired. She is illiterate, speaks Odia, and lives in a rural area. Her lifestyle includes traditional handloom weaving, herbal home remedies, and a disciplined savings habit.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.

GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Uttar Pradesh, district=Kanpur Nagar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Sanskrit.

Patient History and Clinical Summary:
The patient, a 19-year-old male student from Kanpur Nagar, presented with persistent fatigue, recurrent fevers, and unexplained bruising over the past three months. Initial blood work revealed pancytopenia. A subsequent bone marrow aspiration and biopsy confirmed a diagnosis of Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL), B-cell precursor type. The patient was admitted for induction chemotherapy. The treatment plan involves a multi-agent chemotherapy protocol, including vincristine, prednisone, and L-asparaginase, with close monitoring for complications such as tumor lysis syndrome and infections. This is a complex chronic care case requiring prolonged hospitalization and intensive supportive care.

professional_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old home‑based bamboo crafts entrepreneur, blends her mastery of traditional ornaments and household budgeting to launch a small venture that trains local women, yet her spontaneous approach and healthy skepticism often lead her to postpone formal paperwork and miss tight deadlines.
linguistic_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old native Santali speaker, reads and writes in the Ol Chiki script, handles everyday conversations in Hindi, and occasionally grasps English from radio, though her proficiency remains limited.
sports_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old who enjoys casual matches of kabaddi and weekly walks in the paddy fields, occasionally follows the Jharkhand women's Kabaddi team, but prefers solitary fitness through gardening rather than structured gym routines.
arts_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old folk song enthusiast, regularly sings regional folk melodies, weaves bamboo ornaments echoing village motifs, sketches the surrounding fields with a fondness for Chhau dance scenes, and draws inspiration from Baul singer Paban Das Baul and Chhau maestro Suryakant Mishra.
travel_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old who prefers spontaneous short trips to nearby hill stations like Ranchi and Jamshedpur, enjoys weekend pilgrimages to local temples and the sacred grove, and dreams of exploring the cultural richness of Kolkata and Varanasi when finances allow.
culinary_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old who delights in preparing traditional bamboo shoot curry, smoked fish with mustard greens, and simple rice dishes like pakhala, enjoys the tangy flavor of raw mango chutney, but rarely cooks for herself, preferring to share meals with family and neighbors.
persona: Purani Devi, 38, is a curious yet reserved craftsperson who balances family duties with a budding bamboo venture, often leaping into creative projects while hesitating over formal commitments.
cultural_background: Purani belongs to the Santal tribal community of Dumka, Jharkhand, a region where tribal customs intersect with mainstream Hindu festivals. Her family observes traditional Santal rites, such as the Sohrai harvest ceremony, while also participating in Hindu celebrations like Diwali and Navratri. The rural setting, characterized by paddy fields, bamboo groves, and community sangras, shapes a lifestyle that values collective labor, oral storytelling, and reverence for nature.
linguistic_background: Purani's mother tongue is Santali, which she speaks fluently, reads and writes using the Ol Chiki script learned during her primary schooling. She also has functional literacy in Hindi, being able to understand and converse in simple Hindi due to the regional curriculum, though she seldom reads Hindi literature. English proficiency is limited to occasional exposure through radio and television.
skills_and_expertise: Purani excels in household management, preparing traditional Santal meals, budgeting for a large family, and organizing community gatherings. She is skilled in bamboo handicrafts, weaving, and creating folk ornaments. Her knowledge of Santali oral traditions enables her to narrate stories and sing folk songs authentically. She also possesses basic literacy in Ol Chiki and functional Hindi, allowing her to keep simple records and communicate with neighbours.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Household management', 'Traditional Santal cooking', 'Budgeting', 'Bamboo handicrafts', 'Weaving', 'Folk singing', 'Oral storytelling', 'Basic Ol Chiki literacy', 'Functional Hindi communication']
hobbies_and_interests: Purani enjoys singing traditional Santal folk songs, crafting bamboo ornaments, gardening with local vegetables, and listening to radio programmes that discuss tribal arts. She also likes sketching the rural landscape, attending occasional Chhau dance performances, and reading devotional verses in Hindi.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Santal folk singing', 'Bamboo ornament crafting', 'Vegetable gardening', 'Listening to tribal arts radio programmes', 'Sketching rural landscapes', 'Attending Chhau dance performances', 'Reading Hindi devotional verses']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Purani aspires to preserve and revitalize Santal cultural heritage within her village. She hopes to start a small home-based bamboo craft venture that can generate supplemental income for her family while training other women in the community. Additionally, she wishes to become a fluent reader of Hindi literature and eventually assist local schools in teaching Santali folk arts to children.
persona_clinical_summary: Purani Devi, 38, is a retired homemaker from Dumka, Jharkhand, who is married and speaks Santali as her primary language. She has primary education and lives in a rural zone, with functional Hindi literacy. Clinically, she is a reserved individual with a history of traditional Santal customs and may have limited formal healthcare engagement. Her lifestyle includes a sedentary routine with occasional gardening and traditional cooking, and she may have low health literacy.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:

GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;

GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).

  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.

user_prompt:
Write one complete Automated SMS Notifications for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Santali (code=sat, script=Ol Chiki). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jharkhand, district=Dumka.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Ol Chiki for Santali.

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 38
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Jharkhand
- district: Dumka
- first_language: Santali
- clinical_persona_summary: Purani Devi, 38, is a retired homemaker from Dumka, Jharkhand, who is married and speaks Santali as her primary language. She has primary education and lives in a rural zone, with functional Hindi literacy. Clinically, she is a reserved individual with a history of traditional Santal customs and may have limited formal healthcare engagement. Her lifestyle includes a sedentary routine with occasional gardening and traditional cooking, and she may have low health literacy.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
MUST tag [[APPOINTMENT_ID|…]] whenever an appointment is mentioned.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text: [[PATIENT_NAME|Purani Devi]]: Your appointment [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Kumar]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Dumka PHC]] on 21-May at 10:30 AM. Please bring your [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] card. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]].
generated_text_en: [[PATIENT_NAME|Purani Devi]]: Your appointment [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Kumar]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Dumka PHC]] on 21-May at 10:30 AM. Please bring your [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] card. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]].
judge_reasoning: Prose entirely in English; expected Santali Ol Chiki script for SMS body.

professional_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old home‑based bamboo crafts entrepreneur, blends her mastery of traditional ornaments and household budgeting to launch a small venture that trains local women, yet her spontaneous approach and healthy skepticism often lead her to postpone formal paperwork and miss tight deadlines.
linguistic_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old native Santali speaker, reads and writes in the Ol Chiki script, handles everyday conversations in Hindi, and occasionally grasps English from radio, though her proficiency remains limited.
sports_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old who enjoys casual matches of kabaddi and weekly walks in the paddy fields, occasionally follows the Jharkhand women's Kabaddi team, but prefers solitary fitness through gardening rather than structured gym routines.
arts_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old folk song enthusiast, regularly sings regional folk melodies, weaves bamboo ornaments echoing village motifs, sketches the surrounding fields with a fondness for Chhau dance scenes, and draws inspiration from Baul singer Paban Das Baul and Chhau maestro Suryakant Mishra.
travel_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old who prefers spontaneous short trips to nearby hill stations like Ranchi and Jamshedpur, enjoys weekend pilgrimages to local temples and the sacred grove, and dreams of exploring the cultural richness of Kolkata and Varanasi when finances allow.
culinary_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old who delights in preparing traditional bamboo shoot curry, smoked fish with mustard greens, and simple rice dishes like pakhala, enjoys the tangy flavor of raw mango chutney, but rarely cooks for herself, preferring to share meals with family and neighbors.
persona: Purani Devi, 38, is a curious yet reserved craftsperson who balances family duties with a budding bamboo venture, often leaping into creative projects while hesitating over formal commitments.
cultural_background: Purani belongs to the Santal tribal community of Dumka, Jharkhand, a region where tribal customs intersect with mainstream Hindu festivals. Her family observes traditional Santal rites, such as the Sohrai harvest ceremony, while also participating in Hindu celebrations like Diwali and Navratri. The rural setting, characterized by paddy fields, bamboo groves, and community sangras, shapes a lifestyle that values collective labor, oral storytelling, and reverence for nature.
linguistic_background: Purani's mother tongue is Santali, which she speaks fluently, reads and writes using the Ol Chiki script learned during her primary schooling. She also has functional literacy in Hindi, being able to understand and converse in simple Hindi due to the regional curriculum, though she seldom reads Hindi literature. English proficiency is limited to occasional exposure through radio and television.
skills_and_expertise: Purani excels in household management, preparing traditional Santal meals, budgeting for a large family, and organizing community gatherings. She is skilled in bamboo handicrafts, weaving, and creating folk ornaments. Her knowledge of Santali oral traditions enables her to narrate stories and sing folk songs authentically. She also possesses basic literacy in Ol Chiki and functional Hindi, allowing her to keep simple records and communicate with neighbours.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Household management', 'Traditional Santal cooking', 'Budgeting', 'Bamboo handicrafts', 'Weaving', 'Folk singing', 'Oral storytelling', 'Basic Ol Chiki literacy', 'Functional Hindi communication']
hobbies_and_interests: Purani enjoys singing traditional Santal folk songs, crafting bamboo ornaments, gardening with local vegetables, and listening to radio programmes that discuss tribal arts. She also likes sketching the rural landscape, attending occasional Chhau dance performances, and reading devotional verses in Hindi.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Santal folk singing', 'Bamboo ornament crafting', 'Vegetable gardening', 'Listening to tribal arts radio programmes', 'Sketching rural landscapes', 'Attending Chhau dance performances', 'Reading Hindi devotional verses']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Purani aspires to preserve and revitalize Santal cultural heritage within her village. She hopes to start a small home-based bamboo craft venture that can generate supplemental income for her family while training other women in the community. Additionally, she wishes to become a fluent reader of Hindi literature and eventually assist local schools in teaching Santali folk arts to children.
persona_clinical_summary: Purani Devi, 38, is a married homemaker with primary education living in rural Dumka, Jharkhand, who speaks Santali and functional Hindi. She is a retired Santal tribal woman who manages household duties while developing a bamboo craft business. Her lifestyle includes traditional Santal customs and occasional participation in community festivals.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.

MATERNAL SAFETY: clinical content must be age-plausible for persona age=38. Routine ANC/delivery typically ages 18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC/PNC/contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy as a routine story.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jharkhand, district=Dumka.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Ol Chiki for Santali.

- education_level: Primary

She is a retired Santal tribal woman who manages household duties while developing a bamboo craft business. Her lifestyle includes traditional Santal customs and occasional participation in community festivals.

Document format guide for Hospital Billing / Invoice (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.

sports_persona: Bholanath Bhandari is an avid follower of cricket, regularly streaming IPL matches on their mobile and cheering for Kolkata Knight Riders, while they occasionally join a local village cricket team for weekend games.
arts_persona: Bholanath Bhandari enjoys listening to traditional folk songs and occasionally playing the dholak at village gatherings, while they also appreciate Bollywood classics featuring actors like Amitabh Bachchan and listening to contemporary fusion artist Papon.
travel_persona: Bholanath Bhandari prefers solitary walks through fields and nearby forest trails for peace, has visited nearby Durgapur and Kolkata for market trips, and dreams of exploring the hills of Darjeeling and the coastal breezes of Puri.
culinary_persona: Bholanath Bhandari loves the tangy flavor of panta bhat with fried hilsa, enjoys a hearty bowl of machher jhol, and often snacks on bhapa pitha made with rice flour, jaggery, and coconut, though they rarely cook for themselves.
persona: Bholanath Bhandari is a methodical 18‑year‑old loader who blends curiosity‑driven tinkering with disciplined work habits, while they quietly nurture a love for cricket, folk rhythms, and solitary field walks.
cultural_background: Bholanath hails from a Santali tribal family settled in the rural district of Bankura, West Bengal. His community cherishes traditional folk music, dances such as the Santhali drum performances, and celebrates tribal festivals like Karam and Sohrai. Though officially Hindu, his family blends tribal customs with mainstream Hindu rituals, participating in village Durga Puja alongside worship of local forest deities.
linguistic_background: Bholanath's mother tongue is Santali; he is fluent in speaking, reading and writing it using the Ol Chiki script. His middle-school education gave him basic literacy in Hindi, enabling him to understand simple work instructions and fill out routine paperwork. His command of English is limited to a few logistics-related terms he encounters on the job.
skills_and_expertise: Bholanath's core expertise lies in manual loading and unloading of goods in the rural logistics chain. He skillfully handles heavy pallets, estimates weight, and organizes cargo for efficient transport. His methodical approach ensures strict adherence to safety protocols, proficient operation of hand-carts and simple winches, and basic maintenance of loading equipment. He reliably conducts inventory checks and keeps accurate records, often in Santali using the Ol Chiki script. Additionally, his practical curiosity equips him with basic mechanical problem-solving abilities, often employing 'jugaad' to devise quick fixes.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["manual loading and unloading", "weight estimation", "cargo organization", "safety compliance", "hand cart operation", "basic equipment maintenance", "inventory checking", "record keeping (in Santali)", "Ol Chiki script proficiency", "basic mechanical problem-solving (jugaad)"]
hobbies_and_interests: Bholanath enjoys listening to traditional Santali folk songs and, on occasional village gatherings, playing the dholak. He is an avid cricket fan, spending evenings catching matches on his mobile phone. His curiosity drives him to tinker with small mechanical devices, creating improvised tools (jugaad) and solving simple puzzles. He also values solitary walks through the fields, which offer him peace and a chance to observe nature.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["listening to Santali folk songs", "playing the dholak", "watching cricket matches", "tinkering with mechanical devices (jugaad)", "solving simple puzzles", "solitary walks in the fields"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Bholanath aspires to move from a loader to a supervisory position in the logistics sector within the next five years. He plans to enroll in a vocational diploma in supply-chain management and obtain certifications in equipment handling and safety. In the long term, he dreams of setting up his own small transport and loading service that caters to local farms, providing reliable delivery while creating job opportunities for other youths in his village.
persona_clinical_summary: A methodical 18‑year‑old male loader from rural West Bengal, Santali, with middle education, never married, who works in a manual loading role. He is fluent in Santali and basic Hindi, and his lifestyle includes solitary field walks and occasional participation in local cricket. His background is rooted in a Santali tribal family, and he demonstrates disciplined work habits and a competitive streak.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
