Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Santali (code=sat, script=Ol Chiki). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=West Bengal, district=Bankura.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Ol Chiki for Santali.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 18
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Loader and Unloader
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Rural
- state: West Bengal
- district: Bankura
- first_language: Santali
- clinical_persona_summary: A methodical 18‑year‑old male loader from rural West Bengal, Santali, with middle education, never married, who works in a manual loading role. He is fluent in Santali and basic Hindi, and his lifestyle includes solitary field walks and occasional participation in local cricket. His background is rooted in a Santali tribal family, and he demonstrates disciplined work habits and a competitive streak.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- RELIGION — Religion e.g. [[RELIGION|Hindu]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

linguistic_persona: Sitaram Kasekar fluently converses in Sindhi at home, uses Hindi for work and community interactions, reads technical manuals in English with functional proficiency, and understands everyday Marathi, allowing them to navigate Mumbai’s multilingual environment with ease.
sports_persona: Sitaram Kasekar plays street cricket with the Koli Galli Cricket Club on weekend evenings, follows the Mumbai Indians in the IPL, and they maintain personal fitness at a local gym where they focus on strength training and occasional marathon prep runs.
arts_persona: Sitaram Kasekar relaxes by listening to Sindhi folk classics of Bhagat Pannalal, Bollywood melodramas sung by Lata Mangeshkar and Arijit Singh, and they occasionally attend live bhajan concerts featuring Pandit Jasraj, allowing music to color their off‑duty moments.
travel_persona: Sitaram Kasekar enjoys day trips to Lonavala and weekend getaways to Goa with friends, plans occasional pilgrimages to Varanasi during Navratri, and they dream of exploring the Swiss Alps for a blend of scenic photography and cultural immersion, while they prefer a mix of budget‑friendly train travel and occasional flight bookings.
culinary_persona: Sitaram Kasekar, a moderately skilled home cook, delights in preparing traditional Sindhi fare such as sai bhaji with fenugreek leaves, dal pakwan topped with mustard oil, and fresh kachumber made of cucumber, tomato and fresh coriander, while they also savor street foods like vada pav and misal pav, reflecting a palate that balances hearty regional flavors with Mumbai’s street‑food vibrancy.
persona: Sitaram Kasekar blends a practical 'jugaad' flair with tech‑savvy curiosity, never misses a weekend cricket match, hoards pocket‑size railway schematics in a battered notebook, and they occasionally over‑indulge in sweets after festivals, all while keeping a skeptical eye on new ideas.
cultural_background: Sitaram Kasekar is a Sindhi Hindu living in the Mumbai suburban area of Maharashtra. His family originates from the Kutch region of Gujarat, and he retains the Sindhi customs of warm hospitality, lively festivals such as Cheti Chand, and a practical 'jugaad' mindset. The fast‑paced, multilingual urban life of Mumbai blends with these traditions, shaping a balanced outlook that values curiosity for new ideas while relying on proven, practical methods.
linguistic_background: Sitaram grew up speaking Sindhi at home, which he uses daily for family conversations and cultural events. He learned Hindi formally during his schooling and now communicates fluently in both languages at work and in the community. His higher secondary education gave him functional proficiency in English, enabling him to read technical manuals, safety guidelines and railway bulletins written in English. Living in Mumbai also exposed him to basic Marathi, and he can understand and respond to everyday Marathi phrases.
skills_and_expertise: Sitaram is proficient in detailed track inspection, using both manual gauges and handheld track geometry measurement tools to identify wear, alignment and gauge issues. He is skilled in rail welding, metal fabrication and bolt tightening, enabling rapid on‑site repairs under tight schedules. He possesses solid knowledge of Indian Railways safety standards and conducts regular safety audits to ensure compliance with RDSO guidelines. His ability to read and interpret technical drawings and schematics allows accurate execution of maintenance plans. He can troubleshoot basic electrical circuits of signaling equipment and is comfortable using basic computer applications for maintenance logging and reporting. His curiosity drives him to explore newer diagnostic technologies, while his practical mindset keeps him grounded in proven repair methods. He works effectively in teams, coordinating with engineers, supervisors and on‑site crews.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Track inspection', 'Rail welding', 'Metal fabrication', 'Safety compliance', 'Technical drawing interpretation', 'Electrical troubleshooting of signaling equipment', 'Use of handheld track geometry measurement tools', 'Team coordination', 'Maintenance reporting using basic computer applications']
hobbies_and_interests: Sitaram enjoys playing street cricket with friends on weekend evenings, often at the local grounds near his neighbourhood. He loves listening to Sindhi folk songs and Bollywood music, especially during long commutes on the local trains. Cooking traditional Sindhi dishes such as sai bhaji, dal pakwan and kachumber is a relaxing pastime for him. He pursues railway photography, capturing the architecture of stations, tracks and passing locomotives. He regularly attends bhajans at the nearby Shiva temple, especially during Mahashivratri. Keeping fit, he visits a local gym for strength training. In quieter moments, he reads Sindhi folklore collections and occasional railway engineering literature to stay updated.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Playing street cricket', 'Listening to Sindhi folk and Bollywood music', 'Cooking traditional Sindhi dishes', 'Railway photography', 'Attending temple bhajans', 'Gym workouts', 'Reading Sindhi folklore and railway engineering literature']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Sitaram aims to become a senior track maintenance supervisor within the next five years, leading a team and implementing preventive maintenance programs across his division. He plans to obtain a formal railway engineering certification from the Indian Railway Institute of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering to deepen his technical expertise. He is keen on introducing IoT‑based track condition monitoring systems to improve safety and efficiency. Additionally, he wishes to mentor junior technicians, sharing his practical experience while encouraging innovative problem‑solving approaches. His long‑term ambition is to contribute to policy formulation for track safety at a regional level.
persona_clinical_summary: A 32-year-old male track repairer in Mumbai Suburban, Maharashtra, currently married, with higher secondary education. He speaks Sindhi at home and Hindi at work, and is employed in the rail transport sector. His lifestyle includes weekend cricket and a preference for traditional Sindhi cuisine.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Automated SMS Notifications for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Sindhi (code=sd, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Mumbai Suburban.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Sindhi.

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 32
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Track Repairer (Rail Transport)
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Mumbai Suburban
- first_language: Sindhi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 32-year-old male track repairer in Mumbai Suburban, Maharashtra, currently married, with higher secondary education. He speaks Sindhi at home and Hindi at work, and is employed in the rail transport sector. His lifestyle includes weekend cricket and a preference for traditional Sindhi cuisine.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
MUST tag [[APPOINTMENT_ID|…]] whenever an appointment is mentioned.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text: [[PATIENT_NAME|رَمیش کُمار]]: تُہُنٛد سائیکاٹری فالو اپ اپائنٹمنٹ کنفرم چھُ [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240615-02]] پؠٹھ [[HOSPITAL_NAME|سایون ہسپتال]] منٛز ڈاکٹر [[DOCTOR_NAME|ڈاکٹر پٹیل]] سۭتۍ۔ MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0615-001]]۔ مہربانی کٔرِتھ 15 منٹ برونٛہہ وٲتِو۔ کنفرم کرنہٕ خٲطرٕ [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] کال کٔرِو۔
generated_text_en: [[PATIENT_NAME|Ramesh Kumar]]: Your psychiatry follow-up is confirmed for [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240615-02]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sion Hospital]] with Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Patel]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0615-001]]. Please arrive 15 mins early. Call [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] to confirm.

- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Hospital Billing / Invoice for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Sindhi (code=sd, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Mumbai Suburban.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Sindhi.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once (parking/ambulance or motor claim line). Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 32
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Track Repairer (Rail Transport)
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Mumbai Suburban
- first_language: Sindhi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 32-year-old male track repairer in Mumbai Suburban, Maharashtra, currently married, with higher secondary education. He speaks Sindhi at home and Hindi at work, and resides in an urban zone. Clinically, he may have a family history of diabetes and hypertension, and his lifestyle includes a sedentary job with occasional weekend cricket and a diet rich in traditional Sindhi cuisine.

Document format guide for Hospital Billing / Invoice (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (English pivot; wrap facility/geo as tags — never plain-only):
Invoice — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Barddhaman District Hospital]] District [[DISTRICT|Purba Bardhaman]]
PIN [[PIN_CODE|713101]] Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] MRN [[MRN|MRN-WB-2024-0815]]

Landline [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0342-1234567]] Vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|WB40AB1234]] (once)

Receipt No. INV-2024-0815 (plain prose — NOT a TYPE). State line as plain prose (NOT [[STATE|…]]).
Line items with amounts (no entity tags on procedure names).
TYPE aliases forbidden: ADDRESS_NUMBER→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS;
RECEIPT_NUMBER→plain; STATE→plain or DISTRICT.

MANDATORY inline entities (14 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

- TAX_ID — Tax Identification Number (TIN/GSTIN) e.g. [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
- TELEPHONE_LANDLINE — Telephone Number (Landline) e.g. [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PAN_NUMBER — PAN Number e.g. [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

professional_persona: Shilpaben Vasava, a veteran community cultural coordinator and traditional Raso embroidery artisan, blends their keen curiosity with a relaxed, juggad‑style approach to organising temple festivals and mentoring younger women in cooking and stitching, often improvising when schedules slip due to their easygoing nature.
linguistic_persona: Shilpaben Vasava, a fluent oral speaker of their native regional language and equally comfortable in Gujarati, navigates daily life through keen listening and relies on family members for any written communication, while their curious mind constantly picks up new Hindi and English phrases from television.
sports_persona: Shilpaben Vasava enjoys leisurely walks along the Sabarmati Riverfront, attends weekly senior yoga classes, and passionately follows the Baroda cricket team, though they rarely play sports themselves, preferring gentle movement over competitive games.
arts_persona: Shilpaben Vasava delights in listening to traditional folk songs and devotional bhajans, crafting intricate Raso embroidery, and regularly attending cultural performances at the community centre, where they often reminisce about legendary singers like Anup Jalota and folk maestros such as Lalu Ramayana Mishra.
travel_persona: Shilpaben Vasava prefers short family trips to nearby places like Udaipur's lake palace, the Gir wildlife sanctuary, and annual pilgrimages to the holy town of Dwarka, planning each journey with a flexible schedule that leaves room for spontaneous stops, while they often travel by train or shared auto.
culinary_persona: Shilpaben Vasava excels at preparing traditional festival fare such as farsan, sai bhaji, and kesri peda, skillfully using mustard seeds, curry leaves, and jaggery, yet they sometimes rely on shortcuts like pre‑ground masala, and they relish savoring street‑food chaat at the local market during weekend outings.
persona: Shilpaben Vasava is a curious, flexible cultural guardian who blends old‑world crafts with community service, even if their relaxed approach occasionally leads to forgotten appointments.
cultural_background: Shilpaben Vasava belongs to the Sindhi Hindu community that migrated to Vadodara after the Partition. Although her family kept alive Sindhi customs—like celebrating Cheti Chand, singing Sindhi folk songs, and preparing traditional dishes—her daily life is interwoven with Gujarati traditions and the vibrant urban rhythm of Vadodara. She participates in both Sindhi and Gujarati festivals, and she navigates a cultural blend of Sindhi heritage and Gujarati hospitality.
linguistic_background: Shilpaben Vasava is an oral speaker of Sindhi, the language she grew up with, and she is equally comfortable conversing in Gujarati, the lingua franca of Vadodara. Because she never attended school, she cannot read or write in any script, but she can understand basic Hindi and English phrases heard in the neighborhood, television, and from relatives. She uses her strong listening skills to follow religious discourses and community announcements, and she relies on family members for any written communication.
skills_and_expertise: Shilpaben Vasava has honed practical skills through a lifetime of managing a household and nurturing family and community ties. She excels at traditional Sindhi cooking, especially preparing farsan, sai bhaji and sweet dishes for festivals. She knows the art of Raso embroidery and creates intricate patterns on shawls and tablecloths. Her strong oral memory lets her recount family histories and folk stories. She also coordinates community gatherings, like arranging logistics for temple festivals and neighborhood celebrations. Additionally, she is adept at basic home budgeting and caring for elderly relatives.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Traditional Sindhi cooking', 'Raso embroidery', 'Oral storytelling and family history', 'Community event coordination', 'Household management', 'Basic home budgeting', 'Elderly care']
hobbies_and_interests: Shilpaben enjoys listening to Sindhi folk songs and devotional bhajans, cooking special dishes for festivals, and spending time on Raso embroidery. She finds pleasure in tending a modest vegetable garden, strolling through the Sabarmati Riverfront park, and attending cultural programs at the community center. She loves sharing stories and folk tales with her grandchildren and participating in temple aarti. Occasionally, she watches mythological television series and joins local knitting circles.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Sindhi folk music', 'Cooking festival dishes', 'Raso embroidery', 'Gardening', 'Walking in Sabarmati Riverfront park', 'Attending cultural programs', 'Storytelling to grandchildren', 'Participating in temple aarti', 'Watching mythological TV series', 'Knitting circle']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Even at 75, Shilpaben Vasava aspires to be a cultural bridge for her grandchildren and the wider Vadodara community. She wishes to document and pass on Sindhi recipes, folk songs and embroidery techniques, perhaps by dictating her memories to a family member who can transcribe them. She hopes to mentor younger women in traditional crafts, organize small cultural gatherings at the local temple and ensure that the Sindhi heritage remains vibrant in the city. Maintaining good health and staying involved in community service are also central to her personal ambitions.
persona_clinical_summary: Shilpaben is a 75-year-old widowed retired homemaker living in Vadodara, Gujarat, with an illiterate education level. She is an oral Sindhi speaker who also converses in Gujarati and follows a flexible, community-oriented lifestyle. Clinically, she has no reported chronic conditions but may have age-related sensory changes. Her routine includes gentle yoga and walking, with limited formal health engagement.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Insurance Claim / TPA Form for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Sindhi (code=sd, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Gujarat, district=Vadodara.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Sindhi.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once (parking/ambulance or motor claim line). Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 75
- marital_status: Widowed
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Urban
- state: Gujarat
- district: Vadodara
- first_language: Sindhi
- clinical_persona_summary: Shilpaben is a 75-year-old widowed retired homemaker living in Vadodara, Gujarat, with an illiterate education level. She is an oral Sindhi speaker who also converses in Gujarati and follows a flexible, community-oriented lifestyle. Clinically, she has no reported chronic conditions but may have age-related sensory changes. Her routine includes gentle yoga and walking, with limited formal health engagement.

Document format guide for Insurance Claim / TPA Form (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
TPA claim — Policy [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]

MANDATORY inline entities (16 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.

Patient presented with symptoms of severe anxiety and depression following the recent loss of her spouse. Initial assessment revealed symptoms consistent with Major Depressive Disorder, recurrent, moderate severity. Patient reports sleep disturbances, loss of appetite, and feelings of worthlessness. No history of substance abuse or previous psychiatric hospitalization.

Treatment plan includes weekly psychotherapy sessions and initiation of low-dose antidepressant medication. Patient is cooperative with treatment plan and shows good insight into her condition. Family support is available through her daughter who accompanies her to appointments.

Follow-up scheduled in 2 weeks to assess medication response and therapy progress. Referral to psychiatrist for medication management.

professional_persona: Shilpaben Vasava, a veteran community cultural coordinator and traditional Raso embroidery artisan, blends their keen curiosity with a relaxed, juggad‑style approach to organising temple festivals and mentoring younger women in cooking and stitching, often improvising when schedules slip due to their easygoing nature.
linguistic_persona: Shilpaben Vasava, a fluent oral speaker of their native regional language and equally comfortable in Gujarati, navigates daily life through keen listening and relies on family members for any written communication, while their curious mind constantly picks up new Hindi and English phrases from television.
sports_persona: Shilpaben Vasava enjoys leisurely walks along the Sabarmati Riverfront, attends weekly senior yoga classes, and passionately follows the Baroda cricket team, though they rarely play sports themselves, preferring gentle movement over competitive games.
arts_persona: Shilpaben Vasava delights in listening to traditional folk songs and devotional bhajans, crafting intricate Raso embroidery, and regularly attending cultural performances at the community centre, where they often reminisce about legendary singers like Anup Jalota and folk maestros such as Lalu Ramayana Mishra.
travel_persona: Shilpaben Vasava prefers short family trips to nearby places like Udaipur's lake palace, the Gir wildlife sanctuary, and annual pilgrimages to the holy town of Dwarka, planning each journey with a flexible schedule that leaves room for spontaneous stops, while they often travel by train or shared auto.
culinary_persona: Shilpaben Vasava excels at preparing traditional festival fare such as farsan, sai bhaji, and kesri peda, skillfully using mustard seeds, curry leaves, and jaggery, yet they sometimes rely on shortcuts like pre‑ground masala, and they relish savoring street‑food chaat at the local market during weekend outings.
persona: Shilpaben Vasava is a curious, flexible cultural guardian who blends old‑world crafts with community service, even if their relaxed approach occasionally leads to forgotten appointments.
cultural_background: Shilpaben Vasava belongs to the Sindhi Hindu community that migrated to Vadodara after the Partition. Although her family kept alive Sindhi customs—like celebrating Cheti Chand, singing Sindhi folk songs, and preparing traditional dishes—her daily life is interwoven with Gujarati traditions and the vibrant urban rhythm of Vadodara. She participates in both Sindhi and Gujarati festivals, and she navigates a cultural blend of Sindhi heritage and Gujarati hospitality.
linguistic_background: Shilpaben Vasava is an oral speaker of Sindhi, the language she grew up with, and she is equally comfortable conversing in Gujarati, the lingua franca of Vadodara. Because she never attended school, she cannot read or write in any script, but she can understand basic Hindi and English phrases heard in the neighborhood, television, and from relatives. She uses her strong listening skills to follow religious discourses and community announcements, and she relies on family members for any written communication.
skills_and_expertise: Shilpaben Vasava has honed practical skills through a lifetime of managing a household and nurturing family and community ties. She excels at traditional Sindhi cooking, especially preparing farsan, sai bhaji and sweet dishes for festivals. She knows the art of Raso embroidery and creates intricate patterns on shawls and tablecloths. Her strong oral memory lets her recount family histories and folk stories. She also coordinates community gatherings, like arranging logistics for temple festivals and neighborhood celebrations. Additionally, she is adept at basic home budgeting and caring for elderly relatives.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Traditional Sindhi cooking', 'Raso embroidery', 'Oral storytelling and family history', 'Community event coordination', 'Household management', 'Basic home budgeting', 'Elderly care']
hobbies_and_interests: Shilpaben enjoys listening to Sindhi folk songs and devotional bhajans, cooking special dishes for festivals, and spending time on Raso embroidery. She finds pleasure in tending a modest vegetable garden, strolling through the Sabarmati Riverfront park, and attending cultural programs at the community center. She loves sharing stories and folk tales with her grandchildren and participating in temple aarti. Occasionally, she watches mythological television series and joins local knitting circles.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Sindhi folk music', 'Cooking festival dishes', 'Raso embroidery', 'Gardening', 'Walking in Sabarmati Riverfront park', 'Attending cultural programs', 'Storytelling to grandchildren', 'Participating in temple aarti', 'Watching mythological TV series', 'Knitting circle']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Even at 75, Shilpaben Vasava aspires to be a cultural bridge for her grandchildren and the wider Vadodara community. She wishes to document and pass on Sindhi recipes, folk songs and embroidery techniques, perhaps by dictating her memories to a family member who can transcribe them. She hopes to mentor younger women in traditional crafts, organize small cultural gatherings at the local temple and ensure that the Sindhi heritage remains vibrant in the city. Maintaining good health and staying involved in community service are also central to her personal ambitions.
persona_clinical_summary: Shilpaben Vasava, a 75-year-old widowed retired homemaker, is an illiterate Sindhi-speaking woman from Vadodara, Gujarat, who lives in an urban zone. She is a cultural guardian who blends traditional crafts with community service, though her relaxed approach occasionally leads to missed appointments. Her lifestyle includes listening to folk songs, preparing traditional dishes, and mentoring younger women in cooking.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Hospital Billing / Invoice for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Sindhi (code=sd, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Gujarat, district=Vadodara.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Sindhi.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once (parking/ambulance or motor claim line). Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 75
- marital_status: Widowed
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Urban
- state: Gujarat
- district: Vadodara
- first_language: Sindhi
- clinical_persona_summary: Shilpaben Vasava, a 75-year-old widowed retired homemaker, is an illiterate Sindhi-speaking woman from Vadodara, Gujarat, who lives in an urban zone. She is a cultural guardian who blends traditional crafts with community service, though her relaxed approach occasionally leads to missed appointments. Her lifestyle includes listening to folk songs, preparing traditional dishes, and mentoring younger women in cooking.

Document format guide for Hospital Billing / Invoice (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (English pivot; wrap facility/geo as tags — never plain-only):
Invoice — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Barddhaman District Hospital]] District [[DISTRICT|Purba Bardhaman]]
PIN [[PIN_CODE|713101]] Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] MRN [[MRN|MRN-WB-2024-0815]]
Address [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Station Road, Barddhaman]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|anjali.mondal@example.com]] PAN [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
Landline [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0342-1234567]] Vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|WB40AB1234]] (once)
Aadhaar [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|291512345678]] Policy [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-WB-7781]] GSTIN [[TAX_ID|19ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
Receipt No. INV-2024-0815 (plain prose — NOT a TYPE). State line as plain prose (NOT [[STATE|…]]).
Line items with amounts (no entity tags on procedure names).
TYPE aliases forbidden: ADDRESS_NUMBER→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS;
RECEIPT_NUMBER→plain; STATE→plain or DISTRICT.

MANDATORY inline entities (14 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PIN_CODE — ZIP / Postal Code (PIN Code) e.g. [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- AADHAAR_NUMBER — Aadhaar Number e.g. [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
- INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER — Insurance Policy Number e.g. [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
- TAX_ID — Tax Identification Number (TIN/GSTIN) e.g. [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
- TELEPHONE_LANDLINE — Telephone Number (Landline) e.g. [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PAN_NUMBER — PAN Number e.g. [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

persona: Bibhuti Raut is a diligent window cleaner whose artistic curiosity fuels a love for music, sketching and cricket, while their organized mindset and modest frugality keep them grounded, and they stay humble in every task.
cultural_background: Bibhuti Raut hails from a Telugu‑speaking family that settled in the coastal district of Ganjam, Odisha. Growing up in an urban environment, he blends the cultural practices of his Telugu roots—celebrating festivals like Ugadi and Sankranti, enjoying Carnatic music and traditional dance—with the local Odia customs he encounters daily. As a member of the Raut community, he identifies with modest, working‑class values and a strong sense of communal solidarity.
linguistic_background: His mother tongue is Telugu, which he can speak, read, and write at a primary‑school level. Living in Ganjam, he has picked up functional Odia for everyday communication, though he is not formally literate in it. He also understands spoken Hindi from television and local interactions, but his reading and writing skills in Hindi are limited.
skills_and_expertise: His primary expertise lies in window cleaning, where he combines physical stamina with meticulous attention to detail. He is adept at using ladders and safety gear, follows high‑rise safety protocols, and performs routine maintenance of cleaning equipment. Organized and methodical, he plans his daily routes efficiently, manages time well, and ensures consistent service quality. Bibhuti's curiosity about art sharpens his observational skills, enabling him to notice even subtle dust patterns and surface imperfections. He also possesses good interpersonal skills for brief customer interactions, and basic troubleshooting abilities for minor tool repairs.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Window cleaning", "High‑rise safety protocol compliance", "Use of ladders and safety gear", "Routine equipment maintenance", "Time management", "Route planning", "Attention to detail", "Observational skills", "Customer interaction", "Basic tool troubleshooting"]
hobbies_and_interests: Bibhuti enjoys listening to Telugu film songs and Odia folk music, which fuel his love for rhythm and melody. career_goals_and_ambitions: Bibhuti aims to move beyond his current role as a window cleaner by acquiring formal certification in occupational safety and building maintenance. He also hopes to deepen his appreciation for art by attending community workshops whenever possible.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER
